does: This paper develops a framework for training and evaluating large language models as financial trading agents, centered on a curated dataset of multiple-choice questions derived from finance textbooks and historical markets, verified by AI committees and augmented with structured reasoning traces to prevent shortcut learning. The authors introduce a two-stage evaluation protocol that combines isolated MCQ performance assessment with chronological trading simulations across different market regimes. Their experiments demonstrate that open-source models fine-tuned with this approach achieve competitive, risk-aware trading behavior that generalizes beyond the training distribution, outperforms existing open-source baselines, and approaches the performance of frontier models while operating at smaller scale.
connects: This work builds on the emerging literature examining language models as agents for specialized domains, extending prior research on LLM instruction tuning and chain-of-thought reasoning by focusing on the finance domain where ground truth is inherently noisy and performance evaluation is non-trivial. It responds to concerns raised by recent work deploying LLMs as autonomous traders, addressing the critical gap between isolated task performance and real-world generalization. The structured reasoning traces and anti-shortcutting augmentation draw from insights in robustness and out-of-distribution generalization in machine learning, while the chronological simulation protocol adapts evaluation methodologies from quantitative finance research.
enables: This framework enables future development of more reliable autonomous financial decision-making systems by establishing principled methods for training and validating LLM-based agents in markets where ground truth is unavailable. The released dataset and evaluation protocol can serve as benchmarks for assessing financial reasoning in language models across different scales and architectures. The approach points toward broader applications of fine-tuned language models in domains requiring numerical reasoning and long-term sequential decision-making, while opening research directions into understanding how general capabilities transfer to specialized professional contexts and how to design training data that promotes genuine understanding over superficial pattern matching.
